title: Architects of Change: Lynda Resnick
text:
okay welcome everybody to architects
have changed lives conversations with
change makers who are moving humanity
forward and I'm really glad to welcome
someone I really admire who's also a
friend Linda Resnick she is viewed in
the business world as a marketing maven
she is the brains and the heart and the
soul behind some of these products which
I there are more but I just wanted to
put these up in front and center Fiji
which I drink these pistachios these
almonds pom juice Teleflora all of these
companies you may know but that's not
why she's here and she's not here
because she's my friend
she's here because of the work she is
doing in the Central Valley in this
state that caught my eye that inspires
me greatly and that she has really spent
years working on and she said she's
finally ready to let me talk to her
about it so I'm really glad to welcome
Linda to this space and I really want
you guys to pay attention because this
is the story of one woman one company
making a huge difference in one town and
it's something that can be replicated I
believe in the country by other
businesses and in other towns that can
be adopted by really inspirational
leaders like Linda's so welcome
Lin thank you I'm so happy to be here
and you can see if you want to hear her
talk about business and stuff you can
google her and you can hear her at the
Aspen Institute you can hear it all
these other very fancy forums but I want
to talk to you about Lost Hills and your
idea of going into one town which was
barren desolate hopeless and turning it
around where did you get that notion
about nine or ten years ago I realized
that I had to do something besides
writing checks that I was in a position
in life where I could give back in a
meaningful way not just support someone
else's idea but I didn't quite know what
I wanted to do but after six months of
research I decided that the Central
Valley was the place in
most need that related to our lives
because we have 5000 employees there and
I thought if I go in there I know that
they are the working poor and maybe I
could make a difference if I used my
business acumen right to do something
for mankind and I picked the little town
of Lost Hills after a lot of research
and I started focus groups and then home
surveys where I we went into every house
and conducted an in-home survey to find
out what they thought they needed
because how would I know what someone in
lost Hills would need so one this is tip
number one right you went into the homes
to find out what they needed as opposed
to telling them what they needed and
what did you find out Linda that they
told you they needed they wanted well
they were frightened because there were
no lights the streets would be flooded
every time it rained and pray to God it
rains again this year I guess we always
worry about rain the Central Valley not
so awful flood but so we can get the
crop right exactly a new agricultural
state which people often and you should
know that two-thirds of the fruits and
nuts are grown in the Central Valley
when you eat your kale salad be grateful
for the people that are growing the row
crops the tree crops
it's an enormous agricultural center and
so we went to the town they told us
about their fears they told us that how
much they care for their children the
family unit in the Latino communities is
very strong and what do you want first
they wanted a basketball court they
wanted a basketball court because the
boys used to keep their lights on in
their cars so they could play basketball
at night and that was easy to do but one
thing led to another we paved the
streets we put in drought resistant
landscaping we put in the storm drains
and the bus shelters we built a
magnificent park two huge soccer fields
and so forth and so on
I knew that they had to learn to be
empowered that I couldn't stay around
and take care of them for life that you
know it is important for people to feel
a sense of Worth about this work and so
they had to contribute so we started
empowerment classes and we realized that
we had to educate in all sorts of ways
like English as a Second Language
exercise classes nutrition cooking all
sorts of things that we put on at the
community center that we built you took
a very holistic you built an
infrastructure I think that's really
what's important infrastructure is
internal and it's also in the physical
places we live so you built
infrastructure you built places of play
places that would be a community these
are things we used to associate
governments doing local government
building these kinds of things but as a
company and as a woman you came in there
and built what wasn't there exactly you
started trying to build inside of people
right what wasn't there and I realized
five years ago that without education
support it was all going to go to dust
and that's when I started the education
initiative that is actually in 64 school
districts across the Central Valley
where we have programming for all the
employees children that work for us and
we go into their schools and we support
them through their school learning so to
supplement what the public schools are
doing we also built a beautiful campus
in Delano California that now has 1,500
children and next year it's a charter
school a public charter school and next
year we'll have 2,000 children and it'll
be pre-k through graduation do you feel
successful with this and this
- do you feel you've done what you set
out to do your it's complete it's always
a work in progress the education has
been the highlight of my old age now
you've had touch it you know Linda I've
known for a long time
and I also want to just say that in her
philanthropy she's supported the women's
Alzheimer's movement even though it
wasn't kind of in her docket but she
believes in that work so I just wanted
to shout out that and it does obviously
so much more but I think that in your
career that's incredible that you think
kind of if you look at the span of your
career and all the different facets of
it that this is really something you're
the most proud of I would say I'm very
proud of the work that I still do I
still have my day job yes I didn't get
away with that yes and and Halos which I
hope your children are eating because
through the healthiest thing and they'll
be out soon those are the little
clementines you know that's so good no
they're not they're not off the trees
yet but and of course the pomegranates
will be out soon too I can't help but
sell that's just part of my dishes also
very healthy and why oh yes for brain
health
yes thank you you guys that you know
nuts are good for brain health obviously
water and sleep and I know you kind of
look at and you know people also
advocate you know red wine those are all
brain healthy things but what is it
about you know this that makes you feel
like wow I did a good thing well I'm
doing a good thing I mean we have a big
organization I certainly don't do it by
myself
yeah right and we have great educators
the thing is that in the Central Valley
most of the high schools graduate 70 to
80 percent of their kids from high
school we have a hundred percent
graduation rates and all the schools we
serve and 88 percent 88 percent are
going
to a four-year college now this is me
with one year of college at Santa Monica
City College lifelong learning but I
never did graduate college and so for me
to be able to offer this this is
life-changing this is what takes people
out of poverty and into success in life
yeah but it wasn't easy because you have
to educate the parents along with the
children so we start educating them in
preschool and we have classes all the
way through high school for parents but
the big difference that we have in our
education is that our counselors that
are on the high school campus go to the
college campuses we have a different
group there goes to the college campuses
because even though those kids are going
to four-year colleges even Harvard and
Duke and Stanford and Berkeley and UCLA
it's hard for them yeah they're the
first people and their family maybe even
to graduate high school so we spend as
much on taking care of them in college
and high school as we do in educating
them or giving the scholarships we gave
225 college Scholars college
scholarships last year you can cut that
on this yeah and the this next year
we're hoping to give 300 so it's growing
by leaps and bounds and it's fantastic
and now since we started five years ago
we even have some people that just
graduated college coming back to the
Central Valley and I wanted to work in
the Central Valley make it their home
right which is what we pray for because
we want to turn the tide there we want
people to feel empowered so many times
in the public schools they have meetings
during the day in English where the
mommies can't go right and they don't
understand a lot of this goes on in the
valley so why do you think that you know
we live in California right there's so
much focus in California on Los Angeles
on San Francisco on Silicon Valley and
very little on the Central Valley
which really is where our food comes
from where this back-breaking work
continues day in day out
why is it that people know so little
about the Central Valley the only time
they hear about it is when there's a
drought it's also a huge center for oil
exploration there are oil wells
everywhere but as it's just unknown
because the people are not in a position
to advertise that they live there I mean
there's a darling little the boxing boy
I can't remember his name
Xavier anyway that there is a boxer that
did well but you don't see a lot of
famous names coming out of the valley
right and so I think in time that will
change so is it your hope really that do
you want to use the you know work that
you've done in the lost hills community
as an example to other companies to
adopt communities to put in playgrounds
to work in schools what is that I hope
it'll be an inspiration because whatever
you do look around where you work at the
people that are in the neighborhood
where you work at the school they may
just need jerseys for their soccer team
but what a feeling of pride it gives you
to make that contribution we have
something at the wonderful company
called wonderful giving where we give
every employee a thousand dollars yeah
this is gonna ask you about that yeah
for charity for charity so your
philanthropy your philanthropy so you
want your employees to give money to
whatever they think so you give them a
thousand dollars and yes if they'll
contribute it wherever they want and we
hope that they'll contribute it locally
to where they live because we wouldn't
know that they need trees in this little
town or whatever is going on in Oklahoma
because we have offices in Oklahoma and
Texas and other places and
how would we know about that but since
they're on the ground living there they
have contact with the schools and with
the local community and they can do
something that's meaningful or they can
get together as a group and give which
is also great incredible you know gift
and and concept I want to ask you what
keeps you you know as a woman you've
really been in the forefront of business
long before you know so many women were
in this space you've been called the
marketing may then are you trying to
market lost Hills in a way I mean is
that part of this you want to market it
as a town that people know about well
let me tell you about a wonderful story
so and people will know about it I have
some friends that are famous artists
called Simon and Nicky Haas H AAS you
can look at their work on Google and
YouTube and so forth they're brilliant
and whimsical and fun and they were very
upset one day because they were doing a
huge project for Art Basel which is in
early December and in Miami and they
needed beating work done this was
several months ago and they were working
with the ladies in South Africa but the
ladies in South Africa were sassing them
which I thought was great but I didn't
mention that and I and they needed
people to do the work I said I have the
most talented women now they may not do
the beading you're looking for but you
can train them right and so would you be
willing to come to Lost Hills and we'll
put together the group and we'll see
what happens 37 women showed up we ended
up with 14 women coming to the group
they are making $5,000 a month doing
this intricate beading work but it has
done so much that's a lot of money in
lost hills maybe even more than their
husbands making probably it is however
what it's done has brought these 14
women together they talked to each other
they're a support group for each other
they exercise together and when you
interview the women the pride that they
have
contributing to their home in this way
and they can do the work at home minding
the baby cooking dinner and still doing
their beating that's I mean it's just
the greatest thing so tell me Amanda and
congratulations on all of that because
that really is what I call moving
humanity forward that's what an
architect of change does they challenge
what is imagine what can be and then
move humanity forward and you hit all
that criteria what kind of advice would
you have to young women to women who are
wanting to see themselves as
entrepreneurs who want to make money but
also want to build companies that are
socially responsible it's hard in this
day and age especially if you work for a
fortune 500 company and you have to deal
with stockholders and quarterly reports
it is very hard soon and I have always
remained private so we didn't have to
answer to anyone I mean there are crazy
things that we've done we're investing
tens of millions of dollars every year
in our philanthropy in this in the
Central Valley
I don't know that stockholders would be
that excited about that if we were
public because it would be taking money
away from the company but you have to be
true to yourself
and you you really must be authentic I
think as time moves forward in this
space that we are in in America that
authenticity will become more and more
important don't work for a company that
doesn't share your values or for people
you wouldn't want to have dinner with it
sounds silly but it happens we've been
in lawsuits with people that have lied
and the and and drawn their employees in
to the lie or this desperate attempt to
make quarterly earnings look better and
fudge a little on the accounting but
because we've been so hurt in our
society too
by a series of lies and I think that the
new generation the Jen's either's which
are the ones that I have so much faith
in these are the people that are under
like 25
they are the movers and shakers of the
future and in all the research I've done
about them authenticity is the thing
right
that means the most that's the number
one currency and everybody has equal
amounts of that right exactly and so
that's your net worth I always say and I
have written kind of who you work for
and with is far more important than what
you actually do so to have a kind of
lower-level job maybe for someone you
really admire is better than having a
big fancy job with someone who you don't
share their values and you're
embarrassed to say you work for them and
stuff so but to two women you know
there's so many women also boomer women
who are starting businesses who may be
laid off of corporations right
corporations don't want to pay their
health care corporations don't want
quote older employees what advice do you
have for those women about how to stay
in there how to be creative how to you
know honor their life's journey and
their life's experience and feel needed
well it's important to be in touch with
what drives you as a human being for
your for your work it's pretty easy to
find a job today we especially if you
have an education we have very low
unemployment and also there are certain
areas that really need you we need
teachers we need smart teachers
desperately in America I mean
desperately that is an area that I hope
young people and more mature people who
maybe want to go back to the workforce
have a good education could take a few
classes and really start to teach right
healthy a lot of caregivers Oh
caregivers health care yes is in
desperate need so that's something a
caregiver doesn't take the same kind of
training as it does to be a nurse
right and more and more with everything
being in the cloud all the information
you don't really have to go to a doctor
necessarily more and more we're training
people you know we give healthcare free
health care to twelve thousand people in
the valley Wow we give free healthcare
at our clinics I mean at our plants we
have clinics at our plants and mobile
clinics and we're thinking of opening a
primary care clinic in Los Hills now now
that we have a school there that we want
to educate like the women's alzheimer's
movement wants to make sure that primary
physicians are educated about cognitive
health as well as physical health so
also in training caregivers so that's
something that you know the country
desperately needs is educated caregivers
who understand what Parkinson's is who
understand what Alzheimer's is we'll
understand the needs of a special needs
child right who and companies that
understand caregiving needs of their
employees right right right that's so
that's the thing that you know a lot of
women always say to me why I'm you know
I just got laid off of my corporation
they don't want me I'm 55 or I'm 60 what
can I do so those are really good
suggestions yes and I I call it the
Lynda Resnick challenge to companies who
work in communities that are struggling
there's so much you can do you can look
at the lost hills film and look at it as
an example and I believe it's on our
website
yeah and we're gonna have it and we're
gonna post it and what I like is that
it's a work in progress and that you
started with one thing and it's the
needs grew yes you know I I'm I'm boots
on the ground yeah okay heels on the
ground I'm high heels on the ground I'm
I'm little bit tense on the ground I
definitely am and because that's the you
know it's easy to write a check but if
you really get into the community or the
place that you're working you really
understand what the needs are and what
you should do next rather than throwing
money at something doesn't help if you
don't have a firm direction and so it
was all this learning in Lost Hills that
caused us to move out in the valley to
Wasco and Delano
and MacFarlane and Avenel all these
other towns that we're impacting now and
then learning that the incidence of
diabetes is off the charts right and so
what we do our primary focus we do with
all healthcare but our primary focus is
diabetes and we've reduced pre-diabetes
among our employees 42% in two years
that's incredible and also diabetes
those with diabetes you're on track for
Alzheimer's there's a direct correlation
absolutely people who are diabetic or
who are in that space to really also
think about their cognitive health and
get control of their a1c numbers and
also certainly it's also higher in the
Latino community it is there's a
propensity it's diet but it's also
genetics and the ability to metabolize
your lifestyle exactly why
adding community centers adding play
centers getting people moving lighting
sidewalks these are all criteria that
make healthy cities exactly I think
community success and government local
government can do more of that where you
know that's certainly something I think
you've got a good example for what local
government can do and should be doing we
we've we've gotten taxes back to the
town of Los Hills by negotiating for
them so that they pay seventeen million
dollars in taxes so now we're getting a
little bit of our share back in Los
chilsen and the people in Los Hills are
spending this money for what they want
to do and we got the first voting booth
in Lost Hills so I mean that was just a
wonderful thing things we take for
granted every day that they don't have
you know you know I want you all to if
you have time to look at what has
happened in lost hills because of this
woman because it's a time that she's
taken when you drink your Fiji or eat
your pistachios your almonds or order
your flowers think of the work they're
doing and think of the difference a
company can make
think of the difference a community
center and can make think that the
difference play can make lights can make
all of these things a voting booth can
make and so I want to thank you for
coming to talk to you during honor what
you're doing what you've done and you're
going yes Thank You Linda Resnick
remember her name remember that when you
think of these products and remember
that California is an agricultural state
it's much more than Hollywood and
Silicon Valley in San Francisco there
are really great people hardworking
Americans who are living on the brink of
poverty providing food for all of us so
god bless and thank you
Thank You Tara Lee thank you
